---
title: "Safety Car Lottery"
subtitle: "Does SC timing arbitrarily reshape race results?"
---

## The narrative

> *"The safety car is a lottery — its timing is arbitrary and disproportionately reshuffles
> the field, benefiting drivers who happened to pit just before deployment and penalising
> those who just pitted or were running long."*

The hypothesis predicts that races with safety car deployments show materially different
position change patterns to clean races — and that more deployments compound the effect.

---

## Method

Distinct SC, VSC, and red flag deployment events are counted per race from FastF1 race
control messages. These are then correlated with the overtake index (positions gained ÷
starters) for each race.

Data covers **2022–2025** (92 races).

```{python}
import sys
sys.path.insert(0, '../..')

import pandas as pd
import plotly.graph_objects as go
import scripts.export_data as exp
from src.analysis.safety_car import sc_lottery_summary, intervention_frequency, sc_counts_per_race

sc_impact = exp.read('sc_summary_by_race')
results_df = exp.read('results_2022_2025')
sc_df = sc_counts_per_race(results_df)

freq = intervention_frequency(sc_df)
print("SC deployment frequency by season:")
print(freq[['season', 'total_races', 'sc_races', 'sc_rate']].to_string(index=False))
```

```{python}
#| fig-cap: "Left: mean overtake index by SC deployment count. Right: per-race scatter of overtake index vs SC count."

from src.analysis.safety_car import sc_lottery_summary

summary = sc_lottery_summary(sc_impact)

fig_left = go.Bar(
    x=summary['sc_group'],
    y=summary['mean_overtake_index'],
    marker_color=['#2196F3', '#FF9800', '#F44336'],
    text=summary['mean_overtake_index'].round(3),
    textposition='outside',
    customdata=summary['races'].values,
    hovertemplate=(
        '<b>%{x}</b><br>'
        'Mean overtake index: %{y:.3f}<br>'
        'Races: %{customdata}<extra></extra>'
    ),
    name='Mean OI',
)

seasons = sorted(sc_impact['season'].unique())
jitter_x = sc_impact['sc_count'].values
fig_right = go.Scatter(
    x=jitter_x,
    y=sc_impact['overtake_index'],
    mode='markers',
    marker=dict(
        color=sc_impact['season'],
        colorscale='Viridis',
        size=7,
        opacity=0.7,
        colorbar=dict(
            title='Season',
            x=1.0,
            tickvals=seasons,
            ticktext=[str(s) for s in seasons],
        ),
    ),
    customdata=sc_impact[['race_name', 'season', 'round']].values,
    hovertemplate=(
        '<b>%{customdata[0]}</b><br>'
        '%{customdata[1]} R%{customdata[2]}<br>'
        'SC count: %{x}<br>'
        'Overtake index: %{y:.3f}<extra></extra>'
    ),
    name='Per race',
    showlegend=False,
)

from plotly.subplots import make_subplots
fig = make_subplots(
    rows=1, cols=2,
    subplot_titles=['Mean Overtake Index by SC Group', 'Per-Race Scatter'],
)

fig.add_trace(fig_left, row=1, col=1)
fig.add_trace(fig_right, row=1, col=2)

fig.update_layout(
    title=dict(
        text='Safety Car Lottery — Overtake Index vs SC Deployments (2022–2025)',
        y=0.97,
        yanchor='top',
    ),
    template='plotly_dark',
    height=500,
    margin=dict(t=80),
    showlegend=False,
)
fig.update_yaxes(title_text='Overtake index', row=1, col=1)
fig.update_yaxes(title_text='Overtake index', row=1, col=2)
fig.update_xaxes(title_text='SC group', row=1, col=1)
fig.update_xaxes(title_text='SC deployments', row=1, col=2)
fig.show()
```

---

## Findings

| Question | Finding |
|---|---|
| SC deployment rate across 2022–2025? | High overall — peaked at 73% in 2022, fell to 33% in 2024, rose to 50% in 2025 |
| Do SC races produce more position changes than clean races? | Yes — mean overtake index rises from 1.487 (0 SCs) to 1.767 (1 SC) |
| Does a second SC add further position change activity? | Yes — 2+ SCs reaches 2.131, a clear step above 1 SC |
| Is the "lottery" narrative supported? | Yes |

**Verdict: Supported.**

Each additional safety car deployment correlates with a measurable increase in position
change activity, and the effect compounds. A race with two or more safety cars produces
43% more position changes per starter than a clean race (2.131 vs 1.487).

The 2022 season — with a 73% SC rate — stands out as particularly intervention-heavy,
likely reflecting teams' unfamiliarity with the new ground effect cars producing more
incidents. The declining rate through 2024 may reflect stricter deployment thresholds,
faster incident clearance, or simply fewer incidents as teams converged on safer setups.

The data supports the "lottery" framing: SC timing reshuffles the field in ways that are
uncorrelated with underlying race pace.
